section.title: Absalom's Rebellion
section.sectionRationale: The estrangement of chapter 14 erupts into open revolt. Absalom flatters the people, steals their hearts, and has himself proclaimed king at Hebron. David, refusing to defend Jerusalem by force, flees the city in grief, crossing the Kidron and climbing the Mount of Olives weeping and barefoot. He sends the ark back, entrusts himself to the LORD's will, and plants Hushai as a counter-agent against the traitor Ahithophel. The movement is the low point of David's reign, rich with echoes of Christ's own path of rejection and sorrow.
section.thesis: Absalom's cunning flattery steals Israel's heart and drives the anointed king from his own city, yet David flees not in mere despair but in humble submission, sending back the ark and saying, 'let Him do to me whatever seems good,' so that the rejected king, crossing the Kidron and weeping up the Mount of Olives, walks a road that points to great David's greater Son.
section.frameNote: Three beats: Absalom steals Israel's heart and is proclaimed king at Hebron, verses 1 to 12; David flees Jerusalem, and Ittai's loyalty, verses 13 to 23; and the ark sent back and Hushai sent in as David submits to the LORD, verses 24 to 37.

units.label: Absalom steals the kingdom
units.note: he stole the hearts of Israel

verses.text: Some time later, Absalom provided for himself a chariot with horses and fifty men to run ahead of him.

He would get up early and stand beside the road leading to the city gate. Whenever anyone had a grievance to bring before the king for a decision, Absalom would call out and ask, “What city are you from?” And if he replied, “Your servant is from one of the tribes of Israel,”
Absalom would say, “Look, your claims are good and right, but the king has no deputy to hear you.”
And he would add, “If only someone would appoint me judge in the land, then everyone with a grievance or dispute could come to me, and I would give him justice.”
Also, when anyone approached to bow down to him, Absalom would reach out his hand, take hold of him, and kiss him.
Absalom did this to all the Israelites who came to the king for justice. In this way he {{ganab|stole}} the hearts of the men of Israel.
After four years had passed, Absalom said to the king, “Please let me go to Hebron to fulfill a vow I have made to the LORD.
For your servant made a vow while dwelling in Geshur of Aram, saying: ‘If indeed the LORD brings me back to Jerusalem, I will worship the LORD in Hebron.’”
“Go in peace,” said the king. So Absalom got up and went to Hebron.
Then Absalom sent spies throughout the tribes of Israel with this message: “When you hear the sound of the horn, you are to say, ‘Absalom reigns in Hebron!’”
Two hundred men from Jerusalem accompanied Absalom. They had been invited as guests and they went along innocently, for they knew nothing about the matter.

While Absalom was offering the sacrifices, he sent for Ahithophel the Gilonite, David’s counselor, to come from his hometown of Giloh. So the conspiracy gained strength, and Absalom’s following kept increasing.
Then a messenger came to David and reported, “The hearts of the men of Israel are with Absalom.”

And David said to all the servants with him in Jerusalem, “Arise and let us flee, or we will not escape from Absalom! We must leave quickly, or he will soon overtake us, heap disaster on us, and put the city to the sword.”
The king’s servants replied, “Whatever our lord the king decides, we are your servants.”
Then the king set out, and his entire household followed him. But he left behind ten concubines to take care of the palace.
So the king set out with all the people following him. He stopped at the last house,
and all his servants marched past him, all the Cherethites and Pelethites, and six hundred Gittites who had followed him from Gath.
Then the king said to Ittai the Gittite, “Why should you also go with us? Go back and stay with the new king, since you are both a foreigner and an exile from your homeland.

In fact, you arrived only yesterday; should I make you wander around with us today while I do not know where I am going? Go back and take your brothers with you. May the LORD show you {{chesed|loving devotion}} and faithfulness.”
But Ittai answered the king, “As surely as the LORD lives, and as my lord the king lives, wherever my lord the king may be, whether it means life or death, there will your servant be!”
“March on then,” said David to Ittai. So Ittai the Gittite marched past with all his men and all the little ones who were with him.
Everyone in the countryside was weeping loudly as all the people passed by. And as the king crossed the Kidron Valley, all the people also passed toward the way of the wilderness.

units.label: The ark sent back, Hushai sent in
units.note: let Him do what seems good to Him

verses.text: Zadok was also there, and all the Levites with him were carrying the ark of the covenant of God. And they set down the ark of God, and Abiathar offered sacrifices until all the people had passed out of the city.
Then the king said to Zadok, “Return the ark of God to the city. If I find favor in the eyes of the LORD, He will bring me back and let me see both it and His dwelling place again.
But if He should say, ‘I do not delight in you,’ then here I am; let Him do to me whatever seems good to Him.”
The king also said to Zadok the priest, “Are you not a seer? Return to the city in peace, you with your son Ahimaaz, and Abiathar with his son Jonathan.
See, I will wait at the fords of the wilderness until word comes from you to inform me.”
So Zadok and Abiathar returned the ark of God to Jerusalem and stayed there.

But David continued up the Mount of Olives, weeping as he went up. His head was covered, and he was walking barefoot. And all the people with him covered their heads and went up, weeping as they went.
Now someone told David: “Ahithophel is among the conspirators with Absalom.” So David pleaded, “O LORD, please turn the counsel of Ahithophel into foolishness!”
When David came to the summit, where he used to worship God, Hushai the Archite was there to meet him with his robe torn and dust on his head.
David said to him, “If you go on with me, you will be a burden to me.

But you can thwart the counsel of Ahithophel for me if you return to the city and say to Absalom: ‘I will be your servant, my king; in the past I was your father’s servant, but now I will be your servant.’
Will not Zadok and Abiathar the priests be there with you? Report to them everything you hear from the king’s palace.
Indeed, their two sons, Ahimaaz son of Zadok and Jonathan son of Abiathar, are there with them. Send them to me with everything you hear.”
So David’s friend Hushai arrived in Jerusalem just as Absalom was entering the city.

ganab.gloss: To steal, to take by stealth. The narrator says Absalom ganab, stole, the hearts of the men of Israel, through flattery, false promises of justice, and staged humility. The word exposes his rise as theft, not legitimate love won, a subtle, deceptive seizing of loyalty that belonged to the LORD's anointed king.

chesed.gloss: Loving devotion, steadfast covenant loyalty and kindness. David prays that the LORD show chesed to the foreigner Ittai, and Ittai in turn embodies chesed by refusing to leave the rejected king, vowing to share his life or death. In a chapter full of betrayal, this covenant loyalty shines, a faithfulness that mirrors God's own and clings to the true king when the crowd deserts him.

basic.context: The half-healed breach of chapter 14 now bursts open. Absalom launches a patient, calculated bid for the throne. He acquires a royal entourage, stations himself at the city gate, and intercepts those coming to the king for justice, telling each one his case is good but that the king has no one to hear him, and wishing aloud that he were judge. He greets people as equals, kissing them, and so, the narrator says, he steals the hearts of Israel. After four years he gets David's permission to go to Hebron under the pretext of a vow, and there has himself proclaimed king, drawing in even David's trusted counselor Ahithophel. When word reaches David that Israel's heart has turned to Absalom, David chooses not to defend Jerusalem and risk its destruction but to flee. His loyal foreign guard, including Ittai the Gittite who refuses to leave him, goes with him. As the whole company weeps, David crosses the Kidron Valley and climbs the Mount of Olives, head covered and feet bare in grief. Yet his flight is marked by remarkable faith: he sends the ark of God back to the city rather than clutching it as a talisman, submitting himself to whatever the LORD decides, 'let Him do to me whatever seems good.' He prays that Ahithophel's counsel be turned to folly, and sends his friend Hushai back to pose as Absalom's advisor and undermine the traitor. The chapter raises questions about the danger of flattery, the loyalty of the faithful, and the striking submission of the rejected king.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative that both records events and quietly interprets them. Absalom's method is exposed as theft of the heart through flattery and false promises, a warning about charismatic manipulation. David's flight is presented not merely as tactical retreat but as an act of humble submission to God's will, and his sending back of the ark rebukes any superstitious use of holy things. The chapter's many parallels to Christ, the rejected king crossing the Kidron and ascending the Mount of Olives in sorrow, betrayed by a close counselor, are genuine typological resonances the Gospels themselves invite, to be drawn out with care rather than forced.
basic.originalLanguages: The narrator's verdict on Absalom's rise is blunt: he ganab, stole, the hearts of Israel, taking by stealth and flattery a loyalty that was not his to claim. Against that theft stands the chesed, the loving devotion, that David invokes for the foreigner Ittai and that Ittai then embodies, refusing to leave the rejected king even at the risk of his life. In a chapter thick with betrayal, the contrast between stolen hearts and steadfast covenant loyalty is the moral center.
basic.typology: David here walks a road strikingly like the one Christ would walk. The anointed king, rejected by his own people and betrayed by a trusted intimate, leaves the city, crosses the Kidron brook, and ascends the Mount of Olives weeping. Centuries later Jesus, the greater Son of David, betrayed by Judas, would cross the same Kidron to the Mount of Olives, to Gethsemane, in sorrow, and submit Himself to the Father's will. David's 'let Him do to me whatever seems good' anticipates Christ's 'not My will but Yours.'
basic.christ: The rejected David foreshadows the rejected Christ. Both are the true anointed king spurned by the people whose hearts were stolen by another; both are betrayed by a close counselor, Ahithophel and Judas, who each come to a terrible end; both cross the Kidron and climb the Mount of Olives in grief; and both submit to the Father, David saying 'let Him do to me whatever seems good,' Christ praying 'not My will but Yours be done.' David flees to save his people from the sword; Christ goes to the cross to save His people through His own death.
basic.god: God is the one to whom David wholly submits, refusing to secure himself even by clutching the ark, and trusting God to restore him if He delights to, or to do what seems good if He does not. God is thus revealed as the sovereign Lord over the throne, whose favor David will not presume upon or manipulate. And God is the one who answers David's prayer against Ahithophel by providing Hushai, working quietly through human means to overturn the rebellion.
basic.crossReferences: John 18 records Jesus crossing the Kidron to the garden, echoing David's flight. Psalm 3, titled a psalm of David when he fled from Absalom, voices his trust in this crisis. Second Samuel 17 shows God answering David's prayer by frustrating Ahithophel's counsel through Hushai. And Christ's submission in Gethsemane, Luke 22, fulfills David's 'let Him do what seems good.'
basic.oneStory: The anointed king is rejected by his own people, betrayed by a trusted friend, and driven weeping from his city across the Kidron and up the Mount of Olives, yet he submits himself entirely to the LORD's will. This low point in David's story becomes a vivid signpost to Jesus, great David's greater Son, who would tread the same path of rejection, betrayal, and sorrowful submission, and through it win the salvation of His people.

context.literary: The chapter is structured as a fall from the city: Absalom's slow theft of the kingdom, David's sudden flight, and the poignant scenes on the way out, Ittai's loyalty, the weeping at the Kidron, the ark sent back, and the ascent of the Mount of Olives. The narrator interweaves David's faith, his submission and prayer, with his shrewdness, planting Hushai, showing a king who is at once broken and trusting. The geography, Kidron and the Mount of Olives, is loaded with later significance.
context.historical: Sitting at the city gate, where legal cases were heard, let Absalom exploit real grievances about access to royal justice. Hebron, David's first capital, was a fitting and symbolic place to launch the revolt. Ahithophel was David's most respected counselor, and possibly Bathsheba's grandfather, which may have fed his betrayal. The Cherethites, Pelethites, and Gittites were David's professional and foreign bodyguard, whose loyalty contrasts with Israel's defection. Leaving concubines to keep the palace becomes significant in chapter 16.
context.canonical: This chapter is the setting of Psalm 3 and stands behind other psalms of David in distress. Its imagery, the rejected king crossing the Kidron and ascending the Mount of Olives, betrayed by an intimate, is deliberately echoed in the Gospels' passion narratives, making David's darkest hour a prophetic shadow of Christ's. The theme of submission to God's sovereign will, 'let Him do what seems good,' threads through Scripture to Gethsemane and beyond.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To narrate Absalom's deceptive seizing of the kingdom and David's sorrowful flight, portraying David not as a mere loser but as a king who submits himself humbly to the LORD's will, and to set this rejected king's path, across the Kidron and up the Mount of Olives, as a shadow of the greater rejected King to come.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a rebellion and a flight rather than prescribing political conduct; Absalom's flattery is exposed as theft, not modeled. What endures is the warning against charismatic manipulation of the discontented, the beauty of covenant loyalty like Ittai's, and above all David's humble submission to God's sovereign will, which believers are called to imitate and which points to Christ.

debates.question: Was David's decision to flee Jerusalem faith, cowardice, or wisdom?

views.label: Faith and wisdom together, sparing the city and trusting God
views.case: David explicitly fears Absalom will put Jerusalem to the sword, verse 14, and he refuses to make the city a battleground or to clutch the ark as a guarantee, sending it back and submitting to whatever the LORD decides, verse 26; his flight is thus both a wise sparing of the innocent and an act of deep faith, as Psalm 3 shows him trusting God even as he runs, so that leaving is not weakness but humble trust in God's timing and will.

views.label: A tactically sound retreat by a seasoned commander
views.case: Others emphasize the military logic: David was caught unprepared, the people's hearts had turned, and holding an unfortified position against a surging revolt would have been suicidal, so withdrawing to regroup in the wilderness, while planting Hushai and the priests as agents, was the shrewd move of an experienced fighter; even so, the narrative frames the retreat within David's evident submission to God, not mere calculation.

debates.bearing: Both agree the flight is not faithless despair; David spares his city, entrusts his cause to God, and works wisely through Hushai, holding faith and prudence together, and his submission, 'let Him do to me whatever seems good,' becomes the chapter's spiritual high point and its clearest pointer to Christ.

study.originalLanguages: The narrator sums up Absalom's whole campaign in one damning verb: he ganab, stole, the hearts of the men of Israel, verse 6. Through staged humility, flattery, and hollow promises of justice, Absalom took by stealth a loyalty that belonged to the LORD's anointed, so his rise is exposed not as love legitimately won but as theft. Against that dark verb shines the word chesed, loving devotion: David prays that the LORD show chesed to the foreigner Ittai, and Ittai answers by embodying it, refusing to abandon the rejected king and swearing to share his life or death, verse 21. In a chapter saturated with betrayal, stolen hearts on one side and steadfast covenant loyalty on the other, this contrast is the moral center, and it turns the reader's eyes toward the true King whose people's hearts can never be stolen away and whose faithful cling to Him through death itself.

typology.type: The rejected king crossing the Kidron and ascending the Mount of Olives in grief
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who crossed the Kidron to the Mount of Olives and Gethsemane in sorrow the night He was betrayed, John 18:1 and Luke 22:39-44.

typology.type: The king betrayed by a trusted intimate counselor
typology.fulfillment: Christ betrayed by Judas, His own disciple, who like Ahithophel came to a terrible end, Matthew 27:5 and Acts 1:18.

typology.type: The king's submission, 'let Him do to me whatever seems good'
typology.fulfillment: Christ's submission in Gethsemane, 'not My will but Yours be done,' Luke 22:42.

study.christ: The rejected David is a vivid shadow of the rejected Christ. Both are the true anointed king spurned by the very people whose hearts had been stolen away by another; both are betrayed by a close and trusted counselor, Ahithophel and Judas, each of whom came to a dreadful end; both cross the Kidron Valley and climb the Mount of Olives in grief; and both submit themselves wholly to the Father, David saying 'let Him do to me whatever seems good,' verse 26, and Christ praying 'not My will but Yours be done,' Luke 22:42. Yet the parallel deepens into contrast: David fled to spare his people from the sword, while Christ went deliberately to the cross to save His people through His own death, so that the greater Son of David turned rejection itself into redemption.
study.god: God is the one to whom David submits without reserve, refusing to secure his throne even by clutching the ark as a talisman, sending it back and trusting God to restore him if He delights to, or to do whatever seems good if He does not. In this God is revealed as the sovereign Lord of the throne, whose favor cannot be presumed upon or manipulated, and before whom the true response is 'here I am; let Him do to me whatever seems good.' God is also the one who quietly answers David's prayer against Ahithophel, providing Hushai at the summit, working through ordinary human means to overturn the rebellion in His own time. Even in the king's darkest hour, God remains sovereign, faithful, and worthy of trust.

crossReferences.note: David's psalm 'when he fled from his son Absalom,' voicing trust that the LORD is his shield who sustains him in this very crisis.
Jesus crossed the Kidron Valley with His disciples to the garden, echoing David's flight across the same brook.
Jesus went to the Mount of Olives and prayed 'not My will but Yours be done,' fulfilling David's submission, 'let Him do what seems good.'
The LORD answers David's prayer by ordaining that Ahithophel's good counsel be rejected, through Hushai, to bring disaster on Absalom.

study.oneStory: The anointed king is rejected by his own people, betrayed by a trusted friend, and driven weeping from his city, across the Kidron and up the Mount of Olives, yet he submits himself entirely to the LORD's will and works wisely for his people's good. This lowest point of David's reign becomes a striking signpost to Jesus, great David's greater Son, who would tread the same road of rejection, betrayal, and sorrowful submission on the night He was handed over, and who through that path won the salvation of His people. David flees to spare his city; Christ goes to the cross to save the world.

quiz.q: How does Absalom win the people over to his rebellion?

options.text: By flattery and false promises of justice at the gate, so that he stole the hearts of the men of Israel.
options.note: Verses 2-6 describe Absalom's manipulation, summed up as stealing Israel's hearts.

options.text: By defeating David's army in open battle and seizing power.
options.note: There is no battle here; Absalom wins by stealth and flattery, not force.

options.text: By presenting himself as the people's champion for justice.
options.note: True, but this option omits the narrator's pointed verdict that he stole their hearts through deception.

options.text: By inheriting the throne with David's blessing.
options.note: Absalom seizes the throne by conspiracy, not by legitimate inheritance or blessing.

quiz.q: Why does David choose to flee Jerusalem rather than defend it?

options.text: To spare the city from the sword and to submit himself to the LORD's will rather than presume on it.
options.note: Verses 14 and 25-26 show David sparing Jerusalem and submitting to God's decision.

options.text: Because he no longer cared about the throne and gave up in despair.
options.note: David flees in faith and shrewdness, not despair; he plants Hushai and trusts God.

options.text: To avoid bloodshed in the city while trusting God with the outcome.
options.note: True, but this option omits David's striking submission, 'let Him do to me whatever seems good.'

options.text: Because his bodyguard forced him to leave for his safety.
options.note: David decides to leave himself; his loyal guard follows him rather than forcing him out.

quiz.q: What does David do with the ark of God as he flees?

options.text: He sends it back to the city, submitting to the LORD rather than clutching it as a guarantee of safety.
options.note: Verses 24-29 show David returning the ark and entrusting himself to God's favor.

options.text: He takes the ark with him as a talisman to ensure victory.
options.note: The opposite; David refuses to use the ark superstitiously and sends it back.

options.text: He returns the ark to Jerusalem, trusting God to bring him back if He wills.
options.note: True, but this option omits the depth of David's submission, 'let Him do to me whatever seems good.'

options.text: He leaves the ark abandoned in the wilderness.
options.note: David does not abandon the ark; he deliberately sends it back with the priests to the city.

quiz.q: How does the loyalty of Ittai the Gittite stand out in this chapter?

options.text: Though a foreigner newly arrived, he refuses to leave the rejected king, vowing to share his life or death, a picture of covenant loyalty.
options.note: Verses 19-22 show Ittai's steadfast devotion against the backdrop of Israel's betrayal.

options.text: He abandons David like the rest of Israel once the rebellion gains strength.
options.note: The opposite; Ittai clings to David when others desert him.

options.text: He insists on following David even when told to turn back.
options.note: True, but this option omits that he was a foreigner and exile, which makes his loyalty all the more striking.

options.text: He offers David military advice on how to retake the city.
options.note: Ittai's role here is his loyal devotion, not military counsel.

quiz.q: How does David's flight foreshadow Christ?

options.text: The rejected, betrayed king crosses the Kidron and climbs the Mount of Olives in grief and submits to God, as Christ would do the night He was betrayed.
options.note: David's path prefigures Christ's, John 18:1 and Luke 22:39-42.

options.text: It shows that God's true king will always win his throne back by force.
options.note: The typology points to submission and suffering, not victory by force; Christ wins through the cross.

options.text: It pictures a king who submits to God's will in the midst of rejection.
options.note: True, but this option omits the striking geographical and personal parallels, the Kidron, the Mount of Olives, and the betrayal by an intimate.

options.text: It proves that betrayal always ends in the betrayer's triumph.
options.note: Both Ahithophel and Judas come to ruin; betrayal does not triumph, and God overturns the rebellion.

questions: Absalom stole hearts with flattery and empty promises of justice. Where are you vulnerable to charismatic voices that flatter you or promise what they cannot deliver?

David refused to clutch the ark as a good-luck charm and instead submitted to whatever God decided. Where are you tempted to treat holy things or religious habits as a way to control God rather than trust Him?
David prayed, 'let Him do to me whatever seems good.' How hard is it for you to pray that kind of open-handed submission when your future is uncertain?
Ittai the foreigner stayed loyal to the rejected king when Israel deserted him. What would it look like for you to remain faithful to Christ when following Him costs you the crowd's approval?

David's path across the Kidron and up the Mount of Olives points to Christ's own sorrowful submission. How does knowing that Jesus walked the road of rejection and grief before you comfort you in your own hard seasons?

translationNotes.note: The BSB footnote notes that 'four years' follows the Syriac and some Septuagint manuscripts, while the Hebrew reads 'forty years'; four fits the timeline of Absalom's return and rebellion.

David's crossing of the Kidron Valley as he flees is deliberately echoed in John 18:1, where Jesus crosses the same brook to the garden the night He is betrayed, linking David's rejection to Christ's.

David ascending the Mount of Olives weeping, head covered and barefoot, prefigures Christ's sorrow on the same mount in Gethsemane; the covered head and bare feet were signs of mourning and humiliation.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 2 Samuel 15:1-37. Em dashes in the source at verses 18 and 27 were converted to commas; no dashes remain in this file.
